displayTitle: 2026-03-12 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Simon Palmer: Amendment to the Protection of Personal and Property Rights Act 1988
topic: Petition of Simon Palmer: Amendment to the Protection of Personal and Property Rights Act 1988
description: Petition of Simon Palmer: Amendment to the Protection of Personal and Property Rights Act 1988 Simon Palmer Ministry of Justice - Caroline Greaney, Deputy Secretary Policy Carl Crafar, Chief Operating Officer CCS Disability Action - Phoebe Eden-Mann, Policy Analyst Community Law Centres Aotearoa - Karen Hodgson (zoom) Law Reform Coordinator Marianne Startup (zoom) Supervising Solicitor, Marlborough Community Law
descriptionSummary: Petition of Simon Palmer: Amendment to the Protection of Personal and Property Rights Act 1988. Simon Palmer. Ministry of Justice

chunks.text: Good afternoon, everyone. Familiar faces in the back there? Regulars at, uh, petitions committee. Now we what we'd like to see. Alright, thank you. Um, now Mr. Palmer, we do have echo solve it. Well just while we're doing that, um, we can probably introduce ourselves with Echo, um, and then we make sure we fix it up.

So, um, Connor, I chair of the petitions, city Brown. Got it. Greg Clearing, uh, national Party and p for Mongo Garcia, new National mp. Nice to meet you talk Too. Alright, so, um, Mr. Palm, we have you for, um, 10 minutes. So take your petitions, Bert.

Certainly feel free to speak to it, um, the points and hopefully leave time for questions at the end. Thank you. Uh, well and, uh, good afternoon. My name is Simon Palmer and thank you for the opportunity to talk with you today. In 2006, our son was born with a rare chromosome condition, meaning he is fully dependent for every aspect of his life.

I struggle to understand the fairness of the Triple PR Act that discriminates by imposing an annual audit cost. Financially disadvantaging only those individuals whom have a disability. I'm sure you understand that this cohort are unable to manage their own affairs and make their own decisions.

They are the most vulnerable individuals in our community. They need the greatest level of care, support and protection, most of whom earn the least. Property managers currently don't have the ability to engage with an independent auditor and as a result have no control over the associated auditing costs.

There are no upper limits to what can be charged. I therefore don't believe the act is fully protecting the subject person. This is potentially exposing these individuals to exploitation through the charge app model that is currently being used. The corporate charge out model that public Trust uses is well recognized across the corporate and business sector within New Zealand.

Time and attendance is charged at a rate of $247 per hour for any portion of a six minute unit. Detailed on a job sheet I requested from Public Trust for my son's first audit were six separate six minute units. One of those units was a phone call I made to public trust that lasted no more than two minutes and this calculates out at an actual charge out rate of $740 per hour.

Uh, likewise there was a six minute allocation for the generated invoice charge. It's highly unlikely that this would've taken the full six minutes. This model always results in time spent, rounded up to the nearest six minute unit, resulting in an actual charge out rate significantly higher than the $247 per hour rate.

This model does not reflect service cost or cost, uh, recovery methodology as required to be used by public organizations and is generating profit for the public trust and in turn a dividend to the government. Any government seeking to profit from this cohort demonstrates a lack of understanding and care as a corporate model.

Public trust also deals with overdue payments noted on an overdue account I received was, please be aware if this invoice is not paid with them 14 days, we may have no option but to take further steps to recover the outstanding debt. This action may incur collection costs and could negatively affect your credit rating.

That's my son's credit rating. Public trust also offer payment plans for those that can't afford to pay the full amount. A payment plan for an imposed cost that the protected person never asked for and obviously can't afford. Public trust have advised that unpaid property manager examination fees have increased significantly since the subsidy was removed in October, 2024 and sits as of the 31st of January 26 at $352,000.

This corporate model is well understood and accepted by the corporate business sector of New Zealand, however, is not appropriate for this cohort. This corporate model is exploiting the most vulnerable individuals. The model is imposed on low income individuals that would not otherwise engage with corporate services.

As these services for this income group are simply unaffordable as the $352,000 of unpaid fees indicate. There are also some inefficiencies created by lack of training and support resulting in a cost to the protected person. Property managers are moms and dads, uh, grandparents, other relatives or friends of the protected person.

Often the property manager is also the welfare guardian. Most property managers do not come from financial or business backgrounds and find the preparation of accounts for auditing both challenging and stressful. Limited guidance is available however, no training is provided.

This results in incorrect information being supplied to public trust, creating inefficiencies at a significant cost to the protected person. There is currently no ongoing support in place for property managers that may require assistance with the financial management or preparation documents.

If a property manager needs help, surely they should be able to go to an organization without the stress and fear of the protected person being charged. As I've already mentioned, the auditing fee discriminates against those with disabilities. No individual should be subjected to an imposed cost because of how they were born.

The Triple PR act not only covers orders for property managers part four, but enduring power of attorney part nine. Under part four, the property manager must file a set of annual accounts as I do, and the protected person is charged the associated fee under part nine, an enduring power of attorney.

There is no obligation for the person managing the donor's property to file financial accounts and subsequently no fees, no audit fees. This inconsistency is at odds with the intent of the act to provide protection to the most vulnerable individuals.

It discriminates against a cohort by once again financially disadvantaging them through the auditing process and the associated fees and another co cohort by not providing any protection. To conclude, there appears to be a true lack of understanding and care for this cohort and this supporting communities driven by budget constraints and during a cost of living crisis.

In October, 2024, the current government with limited understanding through insufficient consultation with the disability sector removed the subsidy that was available to this cohort. They increased both the asset threshold to 25,000 and the charge out rate to $247 per hour.

Those who now sit below the new $25,000 asset limit and income thresholds no longer have protection. The undetected misappropriation of funds for these individuals would have a significant impact in long-term ramifications. Professional advice I received was to spend my son's money to keep it below the $25,000, uh, limit to avoid the fee.

This is advice I would not give to a non-disabled person, let alone my own son. With unpaid fees increasing 250% over the last 18 months, these latest settings are clearly not working and are simply unaffordable. Speaking from firsthand experience, those who care for the welfare and finances of this cohort work incredibly hard for so many.

It's a thankless job. Day in, day out 24 7, my wife and I have had to prepare for the death of our son on many occasions. He is still very fragile with a short life expectancy, something we've had to live with for 20 years. It is an uncertain future and this uncertainty takes its toll.

There are plenty of others in similar situations to ours. Daily life for many is stressful, full of uncertainty and frustrations. As a country, we have an obligation to support the wellbeing of those who care for this cohort. After all, the court appointed them to these roles. The preparation and submission of financial accounts, along with the usage of the corporate charge out model with no support, uh, stresses these caregivers and property managers do not need the entire disability sector brings significant value

and something incredibly special to our community. Those that interact regularly with this cohort learn and develop traits such as compassion and empathy. They learn kindness and thoughtfulness through both actions and words. They advocate for what is right, all of which has a positive ripple effect throughout our communities.

I am aware that some of you are stepping down later this year and I'm sure that you have all accomplished much in your careers in Parliament. I would ask that you make a positive change to those in your electorate, electorate who are unable to vote. It contribute so positively to the communities they live in by supporting this petition.

Um, so as you know, the petition raises concerns about property orders under the, um, protection of Personal and Property Rights Act or the Triple PR Act.

Um, our submission explained there's two types of property orders. These are the appointment of a property manager, which anyone can have. And the appointment of a property administrator, which only represented people with lower value, income and assets eligible for that type of order is made at the discretion of the court.

Changes made to increase the income and asset thresholds for property administration orders in 2024 meant that more people are now eligible for property administration orders, and those ones are the ones that don't have orders. The petition talks about the cost of financial audits. This relates to the financial statements of the represented person that property managers must submit for auditing by public trust each year.
